title: Sentence Boundaries and Context
concept: Labeling models must look at surrounding context (e.g. capitalizing 'Apple' indicates an organization, not a fruit).

exercises.prompt: How do computer models figure out the correct meaning of a word that has multiple definitions?

options.text: They look at the surrounding words (context).
They only count the number of letters.
They translate the word into numbers and guess.

exercises.explain: Words often have multiple meanings. An AI needs the rest of the sentence (context) to figure out which meaning is correct.

cards.title: The Mystery of Single Words
cards.body: If you see the word 'bank', do you think of money or a river? Without other words around it, you can only guess! AI models face this exact puzzle every day when they try to label words.

cards.title: What is Context?
cards.body: Context refers to the words and punctuation that surround a specific word. Sentence boundaries are the start and end points of a sentence. Labeling models must look at these surrounding clues to understand what a word actually means in that specific spot.

cards.title: The Word Detective
cards.body: Imagine reading the word 'crane'. If you only look at that single word, you don't know if it is a bird, a construction machine, or stretching your neck. But if you see 'the crane lifted the heavy beam,' the surrounding words instantly act like clues to solve the mystery.

cards.title: A Tale of Two Apples
cards.body:
In the sentence: 'I ate an apple.' -> 'apple' is labeled as a Fruit. 

In the sentence: 'Apple released a new phone.' -> 'Apple' starts with a capital letter and is near 'released a phone', so the model labels it as an Organization. Same word, different context!

cards.title: Myth: Words Always Mean One Thing
cards.body: It's easy to think that a word like 'Amazon' always means the rainforest, or always means the shopping website. In reality, models can never assume a word has a fixed label without scanning the entire sentence first.

cards.title: Zooming Out for Meaning
cards.body: See how looking at a single word compares to looking at the whole sentence. The surrounding words change everything!

left.text: The word is 'Apple'. Is it a snack or a tech company? We can't tell!

right.text: 'Apple hired a new CEO.' Now the surrounding words make it clear it's a company!

exercises.prompt: In the sentence 'Apple designs computers,' why does the AI label 'Apple' as an organization instead of a fruit?

options.text: It is always labeled as a fruit, no matter what.
The capital letter and surrounding words show it is a company.
The sentence is too short to tell.
The model randomly guesses when it sees the word.

exercises.explain: Capitalizing 'Apple' and placing it next to 'designs computers' provides the context clues that it refers to a company, not the delicious red fruit.

exercises.prompt: True or False: An AI model can always accurately label a word by looking at that word completely by itself.
exercises.explain: AI models must look at the whole sentence to understand the correct meaning and label of a word.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to complete how AI models read sentences.
exercises.sentence: To label a word correctly, an AI model must look at the surrounding [blank].
exercises.explain: By examining the surrounding words (the context), the AI can tell if 'Turkey' is a country or a bird.

exercises.prompt: Which AI model successfully used context to label the word 'Amazon'?

options.text: AI: 'Amazon (River) shares rose on the stock market today.'
AI: 'Amazon (Organization) shares rose on the stock market today.'
AI: 'Amazon (Fruit) shares rose on the stock market today.'

exercises.explain: Sentence B is correct because the AI looked at the context (capital letter, 'shares rose') to identify 'Amazon' as the online store, not the river. Sentence A and C ignore these context clues.

exercises.prompt: An AI is trying to label the word 'Chase' in a sentence. What is the most important clue it needs to decide if it's a person, a bank, or an action?

options.text: Only the length of the word 'Chase'
The surrounding words like 'Mr.' or 'went to the bank'
Whether the text is written in bold font
The total number of sentences in the entire book

exercises.explain: The surrounding context is the only way a model can tell if 'Chase' is a person's name, an action (to run after), or a bank. Without looking at the nearby words, the model is just guessing!
